anaphora.name: Anaphora
anaphora.plain: Beginning successive clauses, sentences, or lines with the same word or phrase. The repetition builds a drumbeat: each return to the anchor phrase raises the emotional pressure while the changing endings carry the argument forward. It is the signature device of oratory because an audience can feel the structure without seeing it.
anaphora.example: "We shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds, we shall fight in the fields‚Ä¶" ‚ÄĒ Churchill
anaphora.confuse: Epistrophe repeats at clause *ends*; symploce does both at once.

epistrophe.name: Epistrophe
epistrophe.plain: Ending successive clauses or sentences with the same word or phrase. Where anaphora launches each clause from the same spot, epistrophe makes every clause land on the same spot, so the repeated word accumulates finality ‚ÄĒ each sentence hammers the same nail deeper.
epistrophe.example: "‚Ä¶government of the people, by the people, for the people" ‚ÄĒ Lincoln
epistrophe.confuse: Anaphora repeats at beginnings; epanalepsis repeats a clause's opening word at its own end.

symploce.name: Symploce
symploce.plain: Anaphora and epistrophe combined: successive clauses share both their opening and their closing words, with variation trapped in the middle. The frame stays fixed while the center changes, which makes the changing element feel like the only thing in the world that moves.
symploce.example: "When there is talk of hatred, let us stand up and talk against it. When there is talk of violence, let us stand up and talk against it." ‚ÄĒ Bill Clinton
symploce.confuse: Not mere parallelism: symploce requires the identical words at both edges, not just matching grammar.

epanalepsis.name: Epanalepsis
epanalepsis.plain: Repeating a clause's opening word or phrase at its own end, closing the loop: the sentence returns to where it began. The circular shape suggests obsession, inevitability, or a truth that contains itself.
epanalepsis.example: "The king is dead, long live the king."
epanalepsis.confuse: Anadiplosis repeats across a clause boundary (end of one, start of the next); epanalepsis circles within one unit.

anadiplosis.name: Anadiplosis
anadiplosis.plain: The last word of one clause becomes the first word of the next, linking clauses like a chain. Each link hands its weight to the next, so the sequence feels like consequence ‚ÄĒ one thing leading inexorably to another. Stacked anadiplosis ascending in importance becomes gradatio (climax).
anadiplosis.example: "Fear leads to anger. Anger leads to hate. Hate leads to suffering." ‚ÄĒ Yoda
anadiplosis.confuse: Gradatio is anadiplosis sustained up a ladder of intensity; epanalepsis circles back within a single clause.

gradatio.name: Gradatio / Climax
gradatio.plain: A ladder of linked clauses, each picking up the previous one's key word and raising the stakes, until the sequence peaks. The reader climbs step by step, so the summit ‚ÄĒ the final term ‚ÄĒ feels earned rather than asserted.
gradatio.example: "‚Ä¶tribulation worketh patience; and patience, experience; and experience, hope." ‚ÄĒ Romans 5:3‚Äď4
gradatio.confuse: One chained repetition is anadiplosis; gradatio is the whole ascending staircase.

antithesis.name: Antithesis
antithesis.plain: Setting contrasting ideas in balanced, parallel grammar so the opposition itself becomes visible in the sentence's shape. The symmetry implies the two terms exhaust the possibilities ‚ÄĒ the world divides cleanly in two ‚ÄĒ which is why antithesis feels authoritative even when the dichotomy is false.
antithesis.example: "It was the best of times, it was the worst of times‚Ä¶" ‚ÄĒ Dickens
antithesis.confuse: Oxymoron compresses contradiction into a two-word phrase; antithesis holds the contrast across balanced clauses. Paradox asserts a contradiction as true rather than balancing alternatives.

asyndeton.name: Asyndeton
asyndeton.plain: Omitting the conjunctions a list or clause-series would normally carry ("I came, I saw, I conquered" rather than "and I conquered"). The missing connectives compress and accelerate: items arrive as raw succession, implying the list could go on forever or that there is no time for grammar.
asyndeton.example: "‚Ä¶and that government of the people, by the people, for the people, shall not perish." ‚ÄĒ Lincoln (phrases without 'and')
asyndeton.confuse: Polysyndeton is its mirror ‚ÄĒ conjunctions multiplied rather than deleted. Brachylogia is asyndeton between single words.

polysyndeton.name: Polysyndeton
polysyndeton.plain: Multiplying conjunctions beyond grammatical need ‚ÄĒ and‚Ä¶ and‚Ä¶ and ‚ÄĒ so every item in a series gets equal, deliberate weight. The effect can be biblical accumulation, a child's breathless narration, or exhaustion, depending on what is being stacked; the ands flatten hierarchy and make sequence feel like fate.
polysyndeton.example: "And the rain descended, and the floods came, and the winds blew and beat upon that house‚Ä¶" ‚ÄĒ Matthew 7:25
polysyndeton.confuse: Asyndeton deletes the connectives instead. Parataxis is the broader habit of coordination over subordination; polysyndeton is its most audible form.

parataxis.name: Parataxis
parataxis.plain: Placing clauses side by side without subordination ‚ÄĒ no because, although, or which to rank one thought under another. Everything sits on one flat logical plane, so the reader must supply the causal connections the grammar withholds. The signature syntax of Hemingway, the King James Bible, and hard-boiled narration.
parataxis.example: "The sun rose. He walked to the window. The street was empty."
parataxis.confuse: Hypotaxis is its opposite: clauses nested by subordination. Asyndeton concerns missing conjunctions; parataxis concerns missing *hierarchy*.

isocolon.name: Isocolon
isocolon.plain: Successive phrases or clauses of matching length and identical grammatical shape ‚ÄĒ parallelism with the meter of a scale in balance. Because the units weigh the same, the sentence sounds pre-measured, judicial, inevitable; two-part isocolon suggests fairness, three-part suggests completeness.
isocolon.confuse: All isocolon is parallelism, but parallelism only requires matching structure, not matching *length and rhythm*.

parallelism.name: Parallelism
parallelism.plain: Expressing coordinate ideas in coordinate grammatical form ‚ÄĒ matching phrases with phrases, clauses with clauses. The repeated structure tells the reader these items belong to one set and invites comparison among them; break the pattern and the breaking item gets the spotlight.
parallelism.example: "‚Ä¶conceived in Liberty, and dedicated to the proposition‚Ä¶" ‚ÄĒ Lincoln
parallelism.confuse: Isocolon adds equal length and rhythm; antithesis is parallelism put in service of contrast.

chiasmus.name: Chiasmus
chiasmus.plain: A two-part structure whose second half inverts the order of the first: A-B / B-A. The crossing shape (named for the Greek letter chi, X) makes the sentence feel self-sealing ‚ÄĒ it ends by folding back through its own material, leaving nothing outside the pattern. Chiasmus of exact repeated words is antimetabole.
chiasmus.example: "Ask not what your country can do for you ‚ÄĒ ask what you can do for your country." ‚ÄĒ JFK
chiasmus.confuse: Antimetabole reverses the *same words*; chiasmus may invert grammar or concepts without exact lexical repetition.

antimetabole.name: Antimetabole
antimetabole.plain: Repeating the same words in reverse order ‚ÄĒ A-B / B-A with the identical vocabulary. The perfect mirror suggests the speaker has examined the idea from both directions and found the reversal more true; it is chiasmus at its most audible and most quotable.
antimetabole.example: "When the going gets tough, the tough get going."
antimetabole.confuse: Every antimetabole is a chiasmus, but chiasmus can invert structure or sense without repeating the exact words.

polyptoton.name: Polyptoton
polyptoton.plain: Repeating a word in different grammatical forms ‚ÄĒ please/pleasure, judge/judgment, dying/death. The root recurs while its function shifts, so the idea seems to permeate the whole grammar of the sentence: the concept isn't just mentioned, it conjugates through reality.
polyptoton.example: "The Greeks are strong, and skilful to their strength, / Fierce to their skill, and to their fierceness valiant." ‚ÄĒ Shakespeare
polyptoton.confuse: Simple repetition (epizeuxis, diacope) repeats the same form; polyptoton morphs the word as it returns.

epizeuxis.name: Epizeuxis
epizeuxis.plain: Immediate repetition of a word with nothing between: words, words, words. The bare doubling or tripling registers as overflow ‚ÄĒ grief, rage, or emphasis that grammar cannot process, only repeat. The least subtle repetition scheme, and therefore the most visceral.
epizeuxis.confuse: Diacope repeats with a word or two intervening ("O villain, villain, smiling, damn√®d villain"); epizeuxis allows no gap.

diacope.name: Diacope
diacope.plain: Repetition of a word or phrase with a short interruption between the repeats ‚ÄĒ the pattern of "Bond. James Bond." The intervening material gets framed by the echo, and the return of the word feels like insistence: the speaker circles back because once was not enough.
diacope.example: "Put out the light, and then put out the light." ‚ÄĒ Othello
diacope.confuse: Epizeuxis repeats with no gap at all; anaphora repeats only at clause openings.

conduplicatio.name: Conduplicatio
conduplicatio.plain: Picking up a key word from one sentence or clause and redeploying it prominently in the next, dragging the concept forward for re-examination. Unlike a pronoun, the full repetition insists the word itself ‚ÄĒ not just its referent ‚ÄĒ is what matters.
conduplicatio.example: "This afternoon, this Easter afternoon, I saw a friend die. A friend I had known‚Ä¶"
conduplicatio.confuse: Anadiplosis requires the repeat at the exact seam (last word ‚Üí first word); conduplicatio just needs the key word to recur early in the next unit.

metaphor.name: Metaphor
metaphor.plain: Asserting that one thing *is* another, transferring the second thing's properties wholesale without announcing the comparison. Because nothing signals the figure, the reader's mind performs the fusion itself ‚ÄĒ which is why metaphor persuades where argument can't: it installs the mapping before it can be inspected.
metaphor.example: "Juliet is the sun." ‚ÄĒ Romeo and Juliet
metaphor.confuse: Simile flags the comparison with like/as, keeping the two things separate. A metaphor sustained across a passage becomes extended metaphor or allegory; a strained, category-breaking one is catachresis.

simile.name: Simile
simile.plain: An explicit comparison marked by like or as. The marker keeps both terms visible and honest ‚ÄĒ the reader is invited to test the resemblance rather than swallow an identity. Similes can afford to be more outrageous than metaphors precisely because the grammar admits they are figures.
simile.example: "My love is like a red, red rose." ‚ÄĒ Burns
simile.confuse: Drop the 'like' and it becomes metaphor. An epic simile extends the comparison over many lines with its own internal scene.

catachresis.name: Catachresis
catachresis.plain: A deliberately strained or 'wrong' metaphor ‚ÄĒ using a word where no literal word exists, or forcing a term outside its category until the semantic misfit itself is the effect. It reads as slightly broken on purpose; the wrongness defamiliarizes, making the reader re-see an ordinary thing as if newly named.
catachresis.example: "the watery part of the world" ‚ÄĒ Melville, for the ocean
catachresis.confuse: A dead metaphor ('table leg') no longer registers as figurative; catachresis keeps its torque ‚ÄĒ it must still feel misapplied.

metonymy.name: Metonymy
metonymy.plain: Substituting a thing's name with something associated with it ‚ÄĒ the crown for the monarchy, the White House for the administration, the sword for war. The figure works by contiguity (association) rather than resemblance, and it quietly encodes a worldview: what you choose as the stand-in reveals what you take to be the essence.
metonymy.example: "The pen is mightier than the sword."
metonymy.confuse: Metaphor maps by resemblance; metonymy by association. Synecdoche is the special case where the substitute is a *part* of the whole.

synecdoche.name: Synecdoche
synecdoche.plain: Letting a part stand for the whole (hired hands for workers) or the whole for a part (the law for a police officer). The chosen part does silent argumentative work: calling sailors 'hands' reduces them to their labor; calling a car 'wheels' reduces it to motion.
synecdoche.confuse: A subtype of metonymy: synecdoche requires the part/whole relation specifically, not just any association.

personification.name: Personification
personification.plain: Granting human qualities, actions, or feelings to non-human things ‚ÄĒ nations conceive, winds sigh, justice sleeps. It converts description into drama: once a thing can act and intend, it can be a character in the sentence, with motives the reader instinctively reads.
personification.example: "Because I could not stop for Death ‚Äď / He kindly stopped for me ‚Äď" ‚ÄĒ Dickinson
personification.confuse: Prosopopoeia goes further and gives the thing a speaking voice. Pathetic fallacy is personification of nature specifically, colored by the observer's emotion.

prosopopoeia.name: Prosopopoeia
prosopopoeia.plain: Bringing an absent, dead, or non-human speaker onstage to talk ‚ÄĒ giving a voice, not just attributes. Where personification says the storm raged, prosopopoeia lets the storm (or the ancestors, or the country) deliver lines. The borrowed voice carries authority the author could not claim directly.
prosopopoeia.example: Cicero making the state of Rome herself rebuke Catiline.
prosopopoeia.confuse: Personification stops at attributing human qualities; prosopopoeia hands over the microphone.

pathetic-fallacy.name: Pathetic Fallacy
pathetic-fallacy.plain: Nature mirroring human emotion ‚ÄĒ storms for turmoil, spring for hope ‚ÄĒ as if the world felt what the character feels. Ruskin coined it as a criticism (the 'fallacy' of emotional projection), but writers use it deliberately to make setting do the psychological narration.
pathetic-fallacy.example: The storm on the heath matching Lear's madness.
pathetic-fallacy.confuse: Ordinary personification needn't reflect anyone's inner state; pathetic fallacy specifically syncs outer weather to inner weather.

hyperbole.name: Hyperbole
hyperbole.plain: Exaggeration beyond literal credibility, offered in the confidence that the reader will scale it back ‚ÄĒ and land exactly where the writer wanted. The inflation isn't meant to deceive but to transmit intensity: the size of the overstatement measures the size of the feeling.
hyperbole.example: "I had to wait in the station for ten days ‚ÄĒ an eternity." ‚ÄĒ Conrad
hyperbole.confuse: Its opposite pole is understatement/litotes. When exaggeration collapses into the trivial for comic effect, that's bathos.

litotes.name: Litotes
litotes.plain: Affirming something by negating its opposite ‚ÄĒ not bad, no small feat, not unaware. The double processing (reader must flip the negation) slows comprehension just enough to make the understatement felt as a choice: the speaker could have praised directly and pointedly didn't. It projects modesty, irony, or cool control.
litotes.example: "having little or no money in my purse" ‚ÄĒ Melville
litotes.confuse: Meiosis is plain understatement without the negation mechanism; litotes specifically runs on negatives.

meiosis.name: Meiosis / Understatement
meiosis.plain: Deliberately representing something as smaller or less significant than it is ‚ÄĒ calling a wound 'a scratch.' The gap between the thing and its label transfers the unstated magnitude to the reader's imagination, which reliably overshoots; understatement is how stoics boast.
meiosis.confuse: Litotes is understatement achieved specifically through negation ('not unkind'); meiosis just shrinks the label.

irony.name: Irony
irony.plain: A gap between what is said and what is meant (verbal irony), between what a character knows and what the audience knows (dramatic irony), or between expectation and outcome (situational irony). The reader who catches the gap is flattered into complicity with the author ‚ÄĒ irony builds an in-group of those who get it.
irony.example: "Brutus is an honourable man." ‚ÄĒ Antony, meaning the reverse
irony.confuse: Sarcasm is verbal irony weaponized at a target. Paradox asserts a real contradiction; irony merely means other than it says.

paradox.name: Paradox
paradox.plain: A statement that contradicts itself yet insists on being true ‚ÄĒ and, examined, yields a truth ordinary language couldn't hold. Paradox forces the reader to abandon the frame in which the contradiction is impossible; it is the trope of mystics, lovers, and dialecticians.
paradox.example: "The child is father of the man." ‚ÄĒ Wordsworth
paradox.confuse: Oxymoron is paradox compressed to a two-word collision ('living death'); antithesis balances opposites without asserting both are true.

oxymoron.name: Oxymoron
oxymoron.plain: Two contradictory terms fused into one phrase ‚ÄĒ darkness visible, deafening silence. The collision happens inside a single grammatical unit, so the contradiction can't be resolved by reading on; the reader must hold both terms lit at once, which is precisely the state the writer wants to induce.
oxymoron.example: "Parting is such sweet sorrow." ‚ÄĒ Romeo and Juliet
oxymoron.confuse: Paradox is a contradictory *statement* that unfolds; oxymoron is the two-word spark.

allusion.name: Allusion
allusion.plain: An unflagged reference to another text, event, or figure that imports its whole context into the new sentence. The device runs on shared knowledge: readers who catch it receive a second channel of meaning, and the borrowed authority (biblical, classical, literary) arrives without the cost of citation.
allusion.example: "all men are created equal" inside the Gettysburg Address ‚ÄĒ importing the Declaration.
allusion.confuse: Quotation announces its borrowing; allusion smuggles it. Metalepsis is allusion at a further remove, referencing through a chain.

euphemism.name: Euphemism
euphemism.plain: Substituting a mild or indirect expression for a harsh or taboo one ‚ÄĒ passed away, let go, collateral damage. Every euphemism is a small argument about what should not be looked at directly; writers deploy them straight (tact), or expose them ironically to indict the evasion.
euphemism.example: "He is helping the police with their inquiries."
euphemism.confuse: Periphrasis is roundabout wording for any reason; euphemism specifically launders the unpleasant.

periphrasis.name: Periphrasis
periphrasis.plain: Saying something in deliberately more words than needed ‚ÄĒ a roundabout route in place of the direct name. The detour can elevate (epic circumlocution), evade, or characterize a speaker who cannot bring themselves to say the plain thing; the reader always registers the swerve around the missing word.
periphrasis.example: "the finny tribe" for fish, in Augustan verse
periphrasis.confuse: Euphemism detours specifically around the offensive; pleonasm adds redundant words without rerouting the meaning.

pleonasm.name: Pleonasm
pleonasm.plain: Redundancy of words ‚ÄĒ using more than the grammar strictly needs: I saw it with my own eyes. Handled deliberately, the surplus is emphasis or ritual fullness (KJV: 'answered and said'); handled carelessly, it is padding. The effect depends on the reader sensing the extra weight as chosen.
pleonasm.example: "free gift", "burnt fire" ‚ÄĒ or ritual doubling in scripture
pleonasm.confuse: Tautology repeats the same *idea*; pleonasm repeats within one construction. Periphrasis reroutes; pleonasm just doubles.

zeugma.name: Zeugma
zeugma.plain: One verb (or governing word) yoked to two or more objects that require it in different senses ‚ÄĒ often one literal, one figurative. The single verb forks midway, and the reader feels the fork as wit: the sentence economizes and jokes in the same motion.
zeugma.example: "She arrived in a taxi and a flood of tears."
zeugma.confuse: Syllepsis is often used interchangeably; strictly, syllepsis requires the senses to differ while zeugma is any shared-verb yoking.

hendiadys.name: Hendiadys
hendiadys.plain: Expressing one complex idea as two nouns joined by 'and' instead of a noun with modifier ‚ÄĒ sound and fury rather than furious sound. Splitting the idea in two makes both halves stand alone and equal, lending a strange, slightly ritual doubleness that resists paraphrase. Shakespeare's favorite estranging device.
hendiadys.confuse: Not a mere pair of things: in hendiadys the two nouns secretly name *one* thing.

hyperbaton.name: Hyperbaton
hyperbaton.plain: Wrenching normal word order ‚ÄĒ object before subject, adjective after noun, verb delayed ‚ÄĒ so that the displaced word lands with unnatural emphasis. English word order is rigid, which makes every violation loud: the reader feels grammar itself strained by the pressure of the thought.
hyperbaton.example: "Some rise by sin, and some by virtue fall." ‚ÄĒ Measure for Measure
hyperbaton.confuse: Anastrophe is the mild, single-pair inversion; hyperbaton covers more violent or extended derangements.

anastrophe.name: Anastrophe
anastrophe.plain: Inversion of the usual order of a pair of words ‚ÄĒ adjective after noun, preposition after object. A local, controlled reversal that archaizes or poeticizes: the reader hears older English, verse convention, or Yoda, depending on dosage.
anastrophe.confuse: The gentle end of hyperbaton's spectrum; periodic structure delays whole clauses, not word pairs.

periodic-sentence.name: Periodic Sentence
periodic-sentence.plain: A sentence that withholds its main clause (or essential verb) until the end, stacking subordinate material first. The reader is held in suspension, carrying every qualifier forward, until the close snaps the whole structure into meaning at once. It is syntax as delayed gratification ‚ÄĒ and a display of authorial control.
periodic-sentence.example: "Of Man's first disobedience, and the fruit / Of that forbidden tree‚Ä¶ Sing, Heav'nly Muse." ‚ÄĒ Milton
periodic-sentence.confuse: The loose/cumulative sentence is its opposite: main clause first, modifiers trailing after.

cumulative-sentence.name: Cumulative (Loose) Sentence
cumulative-sentence.plain: Main clause first, then modifiers accrete after it ‚ÄĒ participles, absolutes, appositives ‚ÄĒ each adding focus like camera pulls after establishing the shot. The grammar completes early, so everything after feels like generous elaboration; it is the signature sentence of American prose from Twain through Didion.
cumulative-sentence.example: "He dipped his hands in the bichloride solution and shook them, a quick shake, fingers down, like the fingers of a pianist above the keys." ‚ÄĒ Sinclair Lewis
cumulative-sentence.confuse: The periodic sentence withholds its main clause until the end; cumulative delivers it first and then unspools.

parenthesis.name: Parenthesis
parenthesis.plain: An aside inserted mid-sentence ‚ÄĒ set off by dashes, brackets, or commas ‚ÄĒ that interrupts the syntax without joining it. The interruption stages two time-streams at once: the sentence's argument and the mind commenting on it live. It is the printed trace of a speaking voice that can't help itself.
parenthesis.confuse: An appositive renames the noun beside it; a parenthesis digresses without renaming anything.

apposition.name: Apposition / Appositive
apposition.plain: Placing a noun phrase beside another to rename or re-describe it ‚ÄĒ 'my brother, the doctor.' Each appositive is a second take: the writer looks at the same thing twice and hands the reader both exposures. Stacked appositives create incantatory redefinition, each one correcting the aim.
apposition.example: "124 was spiteful. Full of a baby's venom." ‚ÄĒ Morrison (appositive fragment)
apposition.confuse: Parenthesis interrupts without renaming; apposition always re-identifies the neighboring noun.

aposiopesis.name: Aposiopesis
aposiopesis.plain: Breaking off mid-sentence and leaving it unfinished ‚ÄĒ the dash into silence. The unsaid completion is louder than words could be: threat, grief, or the unspeakable, delegated to the reader's imagination. Grammar fails on purpose, and the failure is the meaning.
aposiopesis.example: "I will have such revenges on you both, / That all the world shall‚ÄĒ I will do such things‚ÄĒ" ‚ÄĒ King Lear
aposiopesis.confuse: Ellipsis omits words the reader can reconstruct; aposiopesis abandons the sentence at a cliff edge.

ellipsis.name: Ellipsis
ellipsis.plain: Omitting words that grammar requires but context supplies ‚ÄĒ 'I took the high road; she, the low.' The reader silently restores what's missing, becoming co-author of the sentence. Ellipsis compresses, quickens, and flatters; what it deletes, it also emphasizes by the gap.
ellipsis.example: "And he to England shall along with you." ‚ÄĒ Hamlet
ellipsis.confuse: Aposiopesis breaks off irrecoverably; elliptical omissions are recoverable from the surrounding grammar.

brachylogia.name: Brachylogia
brachylogia.plain: Extreme compression: single words strung without conjunctions ‚ÄĒ a telegraphic staccato. Each word gets a full stress-beat, and the missing grammar reads as urgency, shell-shock, or command. Prolonged, it becomes a style of exhaustion where syntax has been rationed.
brachylogia.example: "We real cool. We / Left school." ‚ÄĒ Gwendolyn Brooks
brachylogia.confuse: Asyndeton omits conjunctions between phrases or clauses; brachylogia does it between bare words.

alliteration.name: Alliteration
alliteration.plain: Repetition of initial consonant sounds across nearby words. It binds words into a perceptual unit before their meanings connect ‚ÄĒ the ear declares them related and the mind scrambles to agree. The oldest structuring device in English verse, and still the fastest way to make a phrase memorable.
alliteration.example: "would sail about a little and see the watery part of the world" ‚ÄĒ Melville's w's
alliteration.confuse: Assonance repeats vowel sounds; consonance repeats consonants anywhere in the word, not just initially.

enjambment.name: Enjambment
enjambment.plain: A verse line that ends before its sentence does, forcing the reader over the line-break to complete the thought. The break creates a split-second of double meaning ‚ÄĒ the line means one thing alone, another once continued ‚ÄĒ and the carried-over word lands with inherited momentum.
enjambment.example: "April is the cruellest month, breeding / Lilacs out of the dead land‚Ä¶" ‚ÄĒ Eliot
enjambment.confuse: An end-stopped line completes its grammar at the break. Caesura is a pause *inside* the line, not at its edge.

in-medias-res.name: In Medias Res
in-medias-res.plain: Beginning in the middle of the action, with context withheld and backstory deferred. The reader arrives late to a world already running and must orient by inference ‚ÄĒ which manufactures instant momentum and flatters the reader into detective work. Epic convention since Homer; the default for modern openings.
in-medias-res.example: Paradise Lost opening after the fall of the rebel angels, not before.
in-medias-res.confuse: Analepsis (flashback) is how the deferred backstory later arrives; in medias res is the choice of entry point itself.

prolepsis.name: Prolepsis
prolepsis.plain: Anticipation: treating a future event as already accomplished, or answering an objection before it is raised. In narrative it is the flash-forward; in argument, the pre-emptive strike. Either way the audience's future is colonized in advance ‚ÄĒ resistance is narrated before it can occur.
prolepsis.example: "Many years later, as he faced the firing squad, Colonel Aureliano Buend√≠a was to remember‚Ä¶" ‚ÄĒ Garc√≠a M√°rquez
prolepsis.confuse: Analepsis looks backward (flashback); prolepsis looks forward. In rhetoric, procatalepsis is the objection-answering subtype.

analepsis.name: Analepsis
analepsis.plain: The flashback: narration steps out of the story's present to recount earlier events. It subordinates chronology to significance ‚ÄĒ the past arrives exactly when it will mean most ‚ÄĒ and reminds the reader that a narrator, not time, is in charge of order.
analepsis.confuse: Prolepsis is its forward-facing mirror, the flash-forward.

apostrophe-device.name: Apostrophe
apostrophe-device.plain: Turning from the actual audience to address someone absent, dead, or something non-human ‚ÄĒ O wild West Wind. The swerve of address dramatizes overflow: feeling too great for the real audience gets aimed at the cosmos. It also quietly recasts the reader as eavesdropper on a private communion.
apostrophe-device.example: "O Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo?"
apostrophe-device.confuse: Direct address speaks to the present audience ('Reader, I married him' sits between the two); invocation is apostrophe petitioning for aid.

invocation.name: Invocation
invocation.plain: A formal appeal to a muse, deity, or power to inspire and authorize the work ‚ÄĒ the epic's opening gesture. It stages humility (the poet claims to be a vessel) while performing supreme ambition, and signals to the reader what tradition the work is claiming descent from.
invocation.confuse: Apostrophe is any address to the absent; invocation specifically petitions for aid at a threshold.

rhetorical-question.name: Rhetorical Question
rhetorical-question.plain: A question posed for effect, whose answer is presumed or performed rather than sought. It conscripts the reader: you supply the answer, and having supplied it, you feel you argued it. A string of rhetorical questions simulates interrogation without surrendering control for a second.
rhetorical-question.example: "Hath not a Jew eyes?" ‚ÄĒ The Merchant of Venice
rhetorical-question.confuse: Hypophora asks and then *answers its own* question; aporia performs genuine (or staged) doubt.

apophasis.name: Apophasis
apophasis.plain: Asserting something by declaring you will not mention it ‚ÄĒ 'I won't dwell on his record.' The denial performs innocence while the content lands anyway; the audience receives the accusation minus the accountability. A favorite of politicians and Mark Antony.
apophasis.example: "I come to bury Caesar, not to praise him."
apophasis.confuse: Paralipsis and praeteritio are near-synonyms; irony is the wider genus of saying-other-than-meaning.

aphorism.name: Aphorism
aphorism.plain: A compact, freestanding statement of general truth, polished until it feels inevitable. Aphorism converts opinion into apparent law: the balance and brevity signal that the sentence has survived long testing, even when freshly minted. Openings that begin aphoristically borrow the authority of proverbs.
aphorism.example: "It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife." ‚ÄĒ Austen (ironic aphorism)
aphorism.confuse: A maxim instructs conduct; an epigram jokes; the aphorism claims truth. Austen's is an aphorism wearing irony.

epithet.name: Epithet
epithet.plain: A characterizing adjective or phrase attached to a name or thing ‚ÄĒ wine-dark sea, swift-footed Achilles. Repeated, it fuses with its noun and becomes identity; the quality stops being observed and starts being definitional. Homeric formula, tabloid nickname, and brand slogan share this engine.
epithet.confuse: A transferred epithet (hypallage) moves the adjective to the wrong noun: 'a sleepless night' ‚ÄĒ the night wasn't sleepless; you were.

hypallage.name: Transferred Epithet / Hypallage
hypallage.plain: An adjective displaced from the noun it logically describes onto a neighboring noun ‚ÄĒ a condemned cell, a restless night. The grammar misassigns the feeling, and the misassignment is expressive: emotion has leaked out of the person and soaked into the furniture of the scene.
hypallage.example: "Once more the storm is howling, and half hid / Under this cradle-hood and coverlid / My child sleeps on" ‚Üí "murderous innocence of the sea" ‚ÄĒ Yeats
hypallage.confuse: A plain epithet sits on its rightful noun; hypallage is the deliberate misdelivery.

enumeratio.name: Enumeration / Accumulatio
enumeratio.plain: Amassing details, examples, or charges in a deliberate pile ‚ÄĒ the catalogue as argument. Sheer quantity becomes quality: by the tenth item the reader stops evaluating and starts surrendering. Whitman's catalogues make abundance into democracy; a prosecutor's make it into guilt.
enumeratio.example: Whitman's catalogues of trades and bodies in "Song of Myself"
enumeratio.confuse: Simple listing is neutral; enumeratio is a list with rhetorical intent ‚ÄĒ the pile *is* the point. Congeries is the near-synonym.

tmesis.name: Tmesis
tmesis.plain: Splitting a word or fixed phrase and wedging another word inside ‚ÄĒ abso-bloody-lutely. The intruder detonates inside the host word, which makes the emphasis feel involuntary, as if feeling burst the word open from within.
tmesis.confuse: Infixation is the linguist's term for the same surgery; parenthesis interrupts a sentence, tmesis interrupts a single word.

bathos.name: Bathos
bathos.plain: A sudden fall from the elevated to the trivial ‚ÄĒ the sublime stubbing its toe. When accidental it is failed grandeur; when engineered, it is deflationary comedy that punctures pretension by letting the reader feel the drop in altitude bodily.
bathos.example: "Here thou, great Anna! whom three realms obey, / Dost sometimes counsel take ‚ÄĒ and sometimes tea." ‚ÄĒ Pope
bathos.confuse: Pathos moves; bathos drops. Anticlimax is the structural name for the same descent.

stichomythia.name: Stichomythia
stichomythia.plain: Dialogue exchanged in rapid, alternating single lines, each speaker capping or reversing the other's words. The tight turn-taking turns conversation into fencing ‚ÄĒ every line parries the last ‚ÄĒ and the shared rhythm shows antagonists locked in one mind even as they oppose.
stichomythia.confuse: Ordinary quick dialogue lacks the line-for-line symmetry and echoed vocabulary that make stichomythia a formal duel.

solecism.name: Solecism
solecism.plain: A deliberate breach of grammar or usage deployed for effect ‚ÄĒ dialect, double negatives, non-standard agreement. In fiction it is voice: the 'error' authenticates a speaker the standard language would erase. What the schoolroom marks wrong, the novelist banks as ethos.
solecism.example: "You don't know about me without you have read a book‚Ä¶" ‚ÄĒ Huck Finn
solecism.confuse: An accidental solecism is just an error; the device requires the wrongness to be chosen and characterizing. Enallage is the classical name for expressive grammatical substitution.

neologism.name: Neologism
neologism.plain: A coined word ‚ÄĒ invented outright or fused from existing parts ‚ÄĒ filling a gap the lexicon didn't know it had. The reader parses it from its components and the surrounding pressure, and the small act of decoding creates ownership: you understand a word no dictionary taught you.
neologism.example: "'Twas brillig, and the slithy toves‚Ä¶" ‚ÄĒ Carroll
neologism.confuse: Catachresis misuses an existing word; neologism mints a new one. A portmanteau is a neologism made by fusion (slithy = slimy + lithe).

enthymeme.name: Enthymeme
enthymeme.plain: A syllogism with a premise left silent, trusting the audience to supply it. The suppressed premise is the persuasive payload: what the audience provides themselves, they do not audit. Most real-world argument runs on enthymemes; finding the missing premise is where critique begins.
enthymeme.example: "He's a politician, so check your wallet." (Suppressed: all politicians are venal.)
enthymeme.confuse: A full syllogism states both premises; an enthymeme's power is exactly the one it doesn't state.

correctio.name: Correctio / Epanorthosis
correctio.plain: Saying something and immediately amending it ‚ÄĒ 'a hundred, no, a thousand times.' The visible self-correction performs sincerity (the speaker cares enough to be exact) while getting both versions on the record; the first term is never really withdrawn.
correctio.example: "Sixty Million and more" ‚ÄĒ Morrison's epigraph revising itself upward
correctio.confuse: Aposiopesis abandons the sentence; correctio revises it in flight and keeps going.

metalepsis.name: Metalepsis
metalepsis.plain: A figure built on another figure ‚ÄĒ reference at a remove, where the reader must traverse a chain of associations to arrive. In narrative theory it is the breach between levels: a character addressing the author, a narrator stepping into the story. Either way, a transgression of the frame that exposes the frame.
metalepsis.example: "I have drunk milk from the cow that scattered the seed" ‚ÄĒ reaching an idea through chained figures
metalepsis.confuse: Simple metonymy makes one associative hop; metalepsis needs at least two. The narrative sense (frame-breaking) is the common modern usage.

free-indirect-discourse.name: Free Indirect Discourse
free-indirect-discourse.plain: Third-person narration that absorbs a character's voice, idiom, and judgments without quotation marks or 'she thought' tags. The narrator's grammar carries the character's mind, so inner life arrives unmediated ‚ÄĒ and irony becomes possible in the gap between the borrowed voice and the narrating intelligence. The great engine of the modern novel, from Austen to Woolf.
free-indirect-discourse.example: "What a lark! What a plunge!" ‚ÄĒ Mrs. Dalloway's mind in the narrator's sentence
free-indirect-discourse.confuse: Direct discourse quotes; indirect discourse reports ('she thought that‚Ä¶'); FID deletes the tag and lets the voices blend.

stream-of-consciousness.name: Stream of Consciousness
stream-of-consciousness.plain: Prose that imitates the unedited flow of a mind ‚ÄĒ associative leaps, unfinished syntax, sensation and memory interleaved without hierarchy. Grammar loosens because the mind being rendered hasn't sorted experience into sentences yet; the reader swims in pre-verbal weather. Joyce, Woolf, and Faulkner made it the modernist signature.
stream-of-consciousness.example: Benjy's section of The Sound and the Fury.
stream-of-consciousness.confuse: Free indirect discourse keeps the narrator's grammatical order while borrowing the character's voice; stream of consciousness lets the order itself dissolve.

passive-voice.name: Passive Voice
passive-voice.plain: The grammatical subject receives the action rather than performing it ‚ÄĒ mistakes were made. Passivization deletes or demotes the agent, and that deletion is always information: modesty, evasion, bureaucratic anonymity, or a world where forces act and no one is responsible. As style, it slows and formalizes; as rhetoric, it hides the doer.
passive-voice.example: "‚Ä¶upon this continent, a new nation, conceived in Liberty" ‚ÄĒ agency diffused into the passive participles
passive-voice.confuse: Not every 'to be' + participle is passive ('he was tired' is adjectival); the test is whether an agent has been displaced.

cleft-sentence.name: Cleft Sentence
cleft-sentence.plain: Splitting a simple sentence into two clauses to spotlight one element ‚ÄĒ 'It was Ishmael who survived' instead of 'Ishmael survived.' The cleft manufactures focus grammatically: the it-clause points, the relative clause carries the rest. English's main tool for stressing a chosen word without italics.
cleft-sentence.example: "It was the best butter." ‚Üí "It was a bright cold day in April‚Ä¶" ‚ÄĒ Orwell's it-frame
cleft-sentence.confuse: A pseudo-cleft uses 'what' ('What he wanted was peace'); both are focus machines, differing in which slot gets the spotlight.

juxtaposition.name: Juxtaposition
juxtaposition.plain: Placing two elements side by side without stated connection and letting the collision generate the meaning. The writer supplies proximity; the reader supplies the relation ‚ÄĒ irony, causation, contrast ‚ÄĒ and owns the inference. Montage in prose.
juxtaposition.example: "The apparition of these faces in the crowd; / Petals on a wet, black bough." ‚ÄĒ Pound
juxtaposition.confuse: Antithesis states its contrast in balanced grammar; juxtaposition just sets the two things down and walks away.

foreshadowing.name: Foreshadowing
foreshadowing.plain: Planting details that quietly predict later events, legible fully only in retrospect. On first pass it tunes mood; on second pass it proves design. Foreshadowing converts a story into a structure ‚ÄĒ the ending was always already present, distributed through the beginning.
foreshadowing.example: Romeo's "my mind misgives / Some consequence yet hanging in the stars."
foreshadowing.confuse: Prolepsis states the future outright; foreshadowing only seeds it. Chekhov's gun is its craft maxim.

symbol.name: Symbol
symbol.plain: A concrete thing that carries a field of abstract meaning beyond its literal presence ‚ÄĒ recurring, accumulating significance each time it returns. Unlike allegory's one-to-one code, a live symbol stays inexhaustible: the green light is longing, the future, money, America, and none of these exactly.
symbol.example: The green light at the end of Daisy's dock ‚ÄĒ Gatsby
symbol.confuse: Allegory fixes the correspondence; metaphor asserts it locally in one sentence. A symbol earns its meanings by recurrence.

archaism.name: Archaism
archaism.plain: Deliberately outdated language ‚ÄĒ four score, thou, brought forth ‚ÄĒ imported for its patina. Old words carry their era's authority with them: scripture, epic, law. The reader hears ancestry, and the sentence claims a place in the long line it echoes.
archaism.example: "Four score and seven years ago‚Ä¶" ‚ÄĒ Lincoln
archaism.confuse: Allusion points to a specific prior text; archaism borrows a period's diction wholesale without citing anyone.

parrhesia.name: Parrhesia
parrhesia.plain: Bold, risky frankness ‚ÄĒ saying the dangerous thing plainly, and often flagging the risk ('I'll speak freely'). The announced candor is itself rhetorical: it buys credibility for everything that follows, since a speaker willing to offend is presumed unwilling to lie.
parrhesia.example: Kent to Lear: "be Kent unmannerly, / When Lear is mad‚Ä¶ What wouldst thou do, old man?"
parrhesia.confuse: Apophasis pretends *not* to say the thing; parrhesia says it and points at its own daring.

metabasis.name: Metabasis
metabasis.plain: A brief transitional summary: what has been said, what comes next. It is the argument turning to face the audience and showing its map ‚ÄĒ an act of confidence that also disciplines the reader's memory into the writer's preferred shape.
metabasis.example: "So much for the causes; now for the consequences."
metabasis.confuse: Metanoia revises a statement; metabasis merely pivots between sections.

amplificatio.name: Amplificatio
amplificatio.plain: Expanding and intensifying a point through repetition, accumulation, and escalation ‚ÄĒ returning to the same idea with greater force each pass. Where enumeration adds instances, amplification adds magnitude: the theme is not just restated but enlarged until it fills the reader's whole field of view.
amplificatio.example: Cicero circling Catiline's treachery in wave after rising wave.
amplificatio.confuse: Enumeratio lists; amplificatio inflates. Auxesis is its close kin, arranging terms in ascending force.

aporia.name: Aporia
aporia.plain: Performed doubt or perplexity ‚ÄĒ the speaker pauses to wonder aloud what to say, how to proceed, whether the thing can be said at all. The hesitation dramatizes honesty and difficulty; sometimes it is real (the essay thinking), sometimes staged (the lawyer 'at a loss' before a jury).
aporia.example: "To be, or not to be, that is the question" ‚ÄĒ deliberation as spectacle
aporia.confuse: A rhetorical question presumes its answer; aporia performs not having one.

onomatopoeia.name: Onomatopoeia
onomatopoeia.plain: Words whose sound imitates their sense ‚ÄĒ buzz, crack, murmur. At full power it extends past single words: whole lines can be engineered so their phonetics perform the event they describe, making the reader's mouth enact the meaning.
onomatopoeia.example: "The moan of doves in immemorial elms, / And murmuring of innumerable bees" ‚ÄĒ Tennyson
onomatopoeia.confuse: Alliteration and assonance pattern sounds without imitating anything; onomatopoeia requires the sound-sense match.

assonance.name: Assonance
assonance.plain: Repetition of vowel sounds within nearby words ‚ÄĒ the deep green sea. Vowels carry a line's music and emotional temperature: long vowels slow and mourn, short vowels patter and quicken. Assonance is rhyme's shy cousin, binding lines below conscious notice.
assonance.example: "in Xanadu did Kubla Khan / A stately pleasure-dome decree" ‚ÄĒ the a's and u's
assonance.confuse: Alliteration repeats initial consonants; consonance repeats consonants anywhere; assonance is vowels only.

mythopoesis.name: Mythopoesis
mythopoesis.plain: Myth-making: narration pitched at the register of origin stories, where characters become archetypes and events become cosmology. The prose signals that what happens here explains the world, not just the plot ‚ÄĒ genealogies, epithets, and ritual repetition are its instruments.
mythopoesis.example: "Many years later‚Ä¶ his father took him to discover ice." ‚ÄĒ Garc√≠a M√°rquez founding a world
mythopoesis.confuse: Allusion borrows an existing myth; mythopoesis manufactures the mythic register itself.

enargeia.name: Enargeia / Vivid Specificity
enargeia.plain: Description so concrete and sensory that the audience seems to witness rather than read ‚ÄĒ the classical virtue of 'bringing before the eyes.' It is precision recruited as persuasion: the detail's specificity vouches for the whole account's truth.
enargeia.example: Homer lingering on the boar's-tusk helmet, strap by strap.
enargeia.confuse: Ekphrasis is enargeia aimed at an artwork; imagery is the broad modern term without the persuasive charge.
